mainEntity.name: Which is better: full-frame or crop sensor?

acceptedAnswer.text: Neither is universally better — it depends on the job. Full-frame excels for low light, maximum dynamic range and shallow depth-of-field (portraits, weddings, night). APS-C/Micro 4/3 shine for reach, lighter kits and lower cost (wildlife, travel, hiking). Choose by subject, working light and how large you print.

mainEntity.name: What is a disadvantage of a full-frame camera?

acceptedAnswer.text: Full-frame systems cost more and are usually bigger/heavier (bodies and lenses). Files are larger, and very fast lenses can give very thin DoF, demanding precise focus. For long hikes or tight budgets, a crop system can be more practical.

mainEntity.name: What is the benefit of a crop sensor?

acceptedAnswer.text: Crop sensors (e.g., APS-C ≈1.5×) offer smaller, lighter, cheaper kits and effectively narrower field of view for the same lens, helping frame distant subjects. Many crop bodies also have high pixel density that resolves fine detail on small subjects.

mainEntity.name: Is a crop sensor better for wildlife?

acceptedAnswer.text: Often, yes. The narrower field of view helps fill the frame, and high pixel density can put more pixels on the bird/animal. Trade-offs: less clean at high ISO than full-frame and shallower dynamic range in difficult light.

mainEntity.name: What does crop factor mean and how does it affect field of view?

acceptedAnswer.text: Crop factor compares a sensor’s diagonal to 35mm (full-frame). It changes field of view, not focal length. 35mm-equivalent focal length = actual focal length × crop factor. Example: 200mm on APS-C (1.5×) frames like a 300mm on full-frame.

mainEntity.name: Does a crop sensor change the lens focal length?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. Focal length is a physical property of the lens. A smaller sensor simply crops the image circle, giving a tighter angle of view that behaves like a longer lens would on full-frame.

mainEntity.name: How does sensor size affect depth of field (DoF)?

acceptedAnswer.text: For the same framing and output size, smaller sensors give more depth of field at the same f-number. To match blur between formats you typically stop the smaller sensor down ~1 stop for APS-C (1.5×) and ~2 stops for Micro 4/3 (2×).

mainEntity.name: Can I use full-frame lenses on APS-C (and APS-C lenses on full-frame)?

acceptedAnswer.text: FF lens → APS-C: Works fine; you see a tighter view (crop factor). APS-C lens → FF: Often vignettes; use your camera’s crop mode if available. Always check the mount and manufacturer guidance.

mainEntity.name: Does full-frame always give sharper images?

acceptedAnswer.text: At the same output size, full-frame is typically cleaner (less noise at high ISO) and holds more highlight detail, but sharpness depends on lens quality, aperture, shutter stability and total megapixels — not sensor size alone.

mainEntity.name: Does a crop body give me extra zoom?

acceptedAnswer.text: Not extra optical zoom — it crops the image circle for a tighter field of view. If your crop body has higher pixel density, you may capture more pixels on the subject than a same-MP full-frame cropped later.
